panel.origin: The first Vice President of the United States (1789–1797), serving under George Washington before becoming the second President.
panel.mixology: Adams established the vice presidency's primary constitutional role as president of the Senate, casting a record number of tie-breaking votes.
panel.examples: Adams famously called the vice presidency 'the most insignificant office that ever the invention of man contrived,' setting an enduring tone of frustration with the role.

panel.origin: The second Vice President (1797–1801), serving under his political rival John Adams before winning the presidency himself.
panel.mixology: Jefferson's service under an opposing-party president exposed a flaw in the original system, where the runner-up became vice president.
panel.examples: The awkward Adams–Jefferson pairing helped prompt the 12th Amendment (1804), which had presidential and vice-presidential candidates run on a single ticket.

panel.origin: The third Vice President (1801–1805) under Thomas Jefferson, and the most notorious figure ever to hold the office.
panel.mixology: Burr killed former Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton in an 1804 duel while still serving as vice president, then was later tried for treason.
panel.examples: His tied electoral vote with Jefferson in 1800, thrown to the House of Representatives, directly spurred the 12th Amendment's reform of presidential elections.

panel.origin: The fourth Vice President (1805–1812), serving under both Jefferson and Madison. A longtime governor of New York.
panel.mixology: Clinton was the first vice president to die in office, and the first to serve under two different presidents.
panel.examples: A leading Anti-Federalist, he had earlier opposed ratifying the Constitution, fearing it gave the federal government too much power over the states.

panel.origin: The fifth Vice President (1813–1814) under James Madison. A signer of the Declaration of Independence whose name became a lasting political term.
panel.mixology: Gerry died in office in 1814, the second vice president to do so, leaving the office vacant for the rest of Madison's term.
panel.examples: As governor of Massachusetts, he approved a district so contorted it resembled a salamander, giving us the enduring word 'gerrymander.'

panel.origin: The seventh Vice President (1825–1832), serving under two presidents before resigning. One of the most formidable political minds of his era.
panel.mixology: Calhoun was the first vice president to resign the office, stepping down to take a Senate seat and champion states' rights.
panel.examples: His fierce advocacy of nullification and slavery made him a towering and divisive figure whose ideas foreshadowed the coming sectional crisis.

panel.origin: The eighth Vice President (1833–1837) under Andrew Jackson, and the first to be elected president directly from the office in modern fashion.
panel.mixology: Van Buren was a master political organizer, credited with helping build the modern Democratic Party and the spoils system.
panel.examples: Born after the Declaration of Independence, he was the first president and vice president who was a citizen of the United States rather than a British subject.

panel.origin: The ninth Vice President (1837–1841) under Martin Van Buren, and the only vice president ever chosen by the Senate.
panel.mixology: When no candidate won an electoral majority in 1836, the Senate elected Johnson under the 12th Amendment's contingency procedure.
panel.examples: He was popularly, if dubiously, credited with killing the Shawnee leader Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames, a claim central to his political fame.

panel.origin: The 15th Vice President (1861–1865), Abraham Lincoln's first-term running mate, dropped from the ticket for Lincoln's 1864 reelection.
panel.mixology: Hamlin's replacement by Andrew Johnson placed Johnson, not the antislavery Hamlin, in the presidency after Lincoln's assassination.
panel.examples: A staunch opponent of slavery, Hamlin is one of history's great might-have-beens, narrowly missing the presidency that Reconstruction would soon define.

panel.origin: The 16th Vice President (1865), who became president just weeks into the term after Lincoln's assassination.
panel.mixology: Johnson is the only person to serve as vice president, then president, and later be impeached, surviving his Senate trial by a single vote.
panel.examples: A Southern Democrat kept on Lincoln's ticket for unity, his clashes with Congress over Reconstruction made his presidency one of the most contentious in history.

panel.origin: The 17th Vice President (1869–1873) under Ulysses S. Grant. A former Speaker of the House caught in a major scandal.
panel.mixology: Colfax is one of only two people to serve as both Speaker of the House and Vice President.
panel.examples: His career collapsed in the Crédit Mobilier railroad bribery scandal, and he was dropped from the ticket for Grant's second term.

panel.origin: The 22nd Vice President (1889–1893) under Benjamin Harrison. A wealthy banker and diplomat.
panel.mixology: Morton had earlier declined the vice-presidential nomination alongside James Garfield, who was assassinated; the man who accepted instead became president.
panel.examples: His decision to refuse the 1880 nomination is a striking historical near-miss, as that very office led directly to the White House.

panel.origin: The 24th Vice President (1897–1899) under William McKinley. An influential adviser who died in office.
panel.mixology: Hobart restored the vice presidency's prestige as a close confidant of McKinley, then died in office in 1899.
panel.examples: His death opened the 1900 vice-presidential slot for Theodore Roosevelt, setting in motion Roosevelt's rise to the presidency the very next year.

panel.origin: The 25th Vice President (1901) under William McKinley, who became president after just six months when McKinley was assassinated.
panel.mixology: At 42, Roosevelt became the youngest person ever to assume the presidency, transforming the office with his energetic, activist style.
panel.examples: Party bosses had pushed him into the 'powerless' vice presidency to sideline him, a plan that backfired spectacularly when McKinley was shot.

panel.origin: The 26th Vice President (1905–1909) under Theodore Roosevelt. A conservative senator from Indiana.
panel.mixology: Fairbanks represented the Republican Party's conservative wing, an ideological counterweight to the progressive Roosevelt.
panel.examples: The city of Fairbanks, Alaska, was named in his honor, a lasting mark from an otherwise quiet tenure in the office.

panel.origin: The 28th Vice President (1913–1921) under Woodrow Wilson, serving two full terms during World War I.
panel.mixology: When Wilson was incapacitated by a stroke in 1919, Marshall declined to assume presidential powers, as no clear procedure then existed.
panel.examples: That constitutional gray area, with the nation effectively leaderless, helped inspire the 25th Amendment's presidential disability provisions decades later.

panel.origin: The 30th Vice President (1925–1929) under Calvin Coolidge. A banker and diplomat with a Nobel Peace Prize.
panel.mixology: Dawes won the Nobel Peace Prize for the Dawes Plan, which restructured Germany's punishing World War I reparations.
panel.examples: He is also the only vice president to have co-written a number-one pop song; his melody later became the standard 'It's All in the Game.'

panel.origin: The 32nd Vice President (1933–1941) under Franklin D. Roosevelt, serving two terms before a bitter falling-out.
panel.mixology: Garner broke with FDR over the court-packing plan and a third term, a rare open rift between a president and vice president.
panel.examples: He is famous for reportedly calling the vice presidency not worth 'a bucket of warm spit,' a blunt verdict on the office's limited power.

panel.origin: The 33rd Vice President (1941–1945) under Franklin D. Roosevelt during World War II, dropped from the 1944 ticket.
panel.mixology: Wallace's replacement by Harry Truman proved pivotal, as FDR died months into the term, making Truman president instead.
panel.examples: A progressive idealist, Wallace's ouster at the 1944 convention is one of history's great turning points, shaping the early Cold War's course.

panel.origin: The 34th Vice President (1945) under Franklin D. Roosevelt, who became president after just 82 days when FDR died.
panel.mixology: Truman was kept so uninformed as vice president that he learned of the atomic bomb only after becoming president.
panel.examples: His abrupt elevation amid World War II underscored the stakes of the vice presidency, thrusting an unprepared man into history's gravest decisions.

panel.origin: The 35th Vice President (1949–1953) under Harry Truman. A beloved Senate veteran who coined the office's nickname.
panel.mixology: Barkley's grandson suggested the nickname 'Veep' for the office, a term that has endured ever since.
panel.examples: At 71 the oldest man to take the office at the time, his folksy charm helped rehabilitate the vice presidency's public image.

panel.origin: The 36th Vice President (1953–1961) under Dwight Eisenhower, who later became president himself in 1969.
panel.mixology: Nixon expanded the vice presidency's visibility, taking on diplomatic missions and a far more public role than predecessors.
panel.examples: His 'Checkers speech,' saving his spot on the 1952 ticket amid a funds scandal, was a pioneering use of television in American politics.

panel.origin: The 37th Vice President (1961–1963) under John F. Kennedy, who became president after Kennedy's assassination.
panel.mixology: Johnson took the oath aboard Air Force One hours after Kennedy was shot, then drove landmark civil rights legislation through Congress.
panel.examples: A legendary Senate majority leader before the office, his masterful command of legislative power defined his consequential presidency.

panel.origin: The 38th Vice President (1965–1969) under Lyndon Johnson, and the Democratic nominee who narrowly lost the 1968 presidential race.
panel.mixology: Humphrey championed civil rights for decades, having electrified the 1948 convention with a landmark speech on the issue.
panel.examples: His loyalty to Johnson's Vietnam policy as vice president hampered his own 1968 campaign, a cautionary tale of the office's constraints.

panel.origin: The 40th Vice President (1973–1974), the first appointed under the 25th Amendment, who then became the only unelected president.
panel.mixology: Ford was appointed after Spiro Agnew resigned, then became president when Nixon resigned, never having been elected to either office.
panel.examples: His unprecedented path to the White House, entirely through appointment and resignation, was a unique test of the 25th Amendment's machinery.

panel.origin: The 42nd Vice President (1977–1981) under Jimmy Carter, who transformed the office into a genuine governing partnership.
panel.mixology: Mondale is credited with redefining the modern vice presidency, securing a West Wing office and a true advisory role for the first time.
panel.examples: His partnership model with Carter became the template every subsequent vice president has followed, turning a ceremonial post into real influence.

panel.origin: The 43rd Vice President (1981–1989) under Ronald Reagan, who then won the presidency in 1988.
panel.mixology: Bush was the first sitting vice president since Martin Van Buren in 1836 to be elected directly to the presidency.
panel.examples: His broad résumé as the office's holder, from the CIA to the UN, exemplified the modern vice presidency as a launchpad to the top job.

panel.origin: The 45th Vice President (1993–2001) under Bill Clinton, and the central figure in one of the closest elections in history.
panel.mixology: Gore won the national popular vote in 2000 but lost the presidency after a Supreme Court ruling halted the Florida recount.
panel.examples: He later won the Nobel Peace Prize and an Academy Award for his climate-change advocacy, a remarkable post-office second act.

panel.origin: The 46th Vice President (2001–2009) under George W. Bush, widely regarded as among the most powerful ever to hold the office.
panel.mixology: Cheney wielded unprecedented influence over national security and foreign policy, especially after the September 11 attacks.
panel.examples: His expansive vision of vice-presidential and executive power made him a defining, and deeply debated, figure of the post-9/11 era.

panel.origin: The 47th Vice President (2009–2017) under Barack Obama, who later won the presidency himself in 2020.
panel.mixology: Biden drew on decades of Senate experience to serve as a key legislative negotiator and adviser throughout Obama's two terms.
panel.examples: His path from the office to the presidency four years later echoed a route walked by Nixon, marking him among the most experienced figures in modern politics.

panel.origin: The 49th Vice President (2021–2025) under Joe Biden, the first woman and first person of South Asian and Black heritage in the office.
panel.mixology: Harris broke a historic barrier as the first female vice president, and as a senator had cast tie-breaking votes of unusual frequency.
panel.examples: A former senator and California attorney general, her election marked a milestone in the long expansion of who could hold national executive office.

FOUNDING ERA.en: John Adams found the vice presidency so frustrating he wrote that it was the most insignificant office ever invented.

19TH CENTURY.en: Two vice presidents in this era — Calhoun and later others — left the office in dramatic fashion, one by resignation.

TURN OF THE CENTURY.en: Theodore Roosevelt was pushed into the vice presidency to sideline him, only to become president within months.

MODERN ERA.en: Harry Truman was so out of the loop as VP that he didn't know about the atomic bomb until he became president.

CONTEMPORARY.en: Walter Mondale reinvented the modern vice presidency, winning a West Wing office and a real governing role.
